body_txt: The rural southern U.S. , parts of the Great Plains and Rocky Mountains , and Appalachia , and indeed, the rural north , east and west of England , are apparently full of small towns run by evil hicks of some sort . Their control over the town may be political, economic, religious , or purely criminal , but in most cases it gradually expands to "all of the above". One of the most obvious hallmarks of a town run by a Tyrant is the apparent lack of a judicial system, oftentimes because they're The Sheriff and take this role to mean Judge, Jury, and Executioner . It seems the Tyrant can just go around arresting whomever they want for no reason without them ever getting a trial. If there's a courthouse in town, it's certainly not being used. (On the rare occasion there is a trial, it will have a Hanging Judge who will be on the payroll , if not said tyrant themself once again.) In all these respects, this trope is effectively a modern-day version of the Feudal Overlord of yore (and they may even act like it too, demanding they be referred to with noble titles and such). The fact that they were probably elected due to his popularity will usually be ignored, though expect them to be a Corrupt Politician who buys their elections and/or makes sure they're the only name on the ballot if this comes up. It could also be that the locals in the small town genuinely like (or at least, respect) their strongman approach to things, or they have genuinely done things to improve the town since gaining power , justifying their continued presence. And of course, it could be that they're more dirty work is simply kept under wraps so the voters simply don't know better. They will almost certainly wear a hat , probably carry a gun (at least in American versions), will probably chew tobacco and/or smoke cigars , is almost always white, and is virtually (though not guaranteed to be) Always Male (the female examples of this trope are rare and not only forced to prove they are More Deadly Than the Male to keep their power but usually have a history of having inherited — or " inherited " — the role from a male version of this trope who set the foundation of this empire ). Will typically have a uniquely Southern given name akin to Rufus or Clement . Good odds of being a Fat, Sweaty Southerner in a White Suit and/or a Half-Witted Hillbilly , and if they're the mayor of the town, they will most likely be an evil Mayor Pain . There is also a good chance that they are or related to a Klansman if they're from the Deep South . If they're not a cop or involved in local politics, odds are that they're a businessperson , especially a Cattle Baron or Railroad Baron in period settings, who doesn't want those irritating townsfolk getting in the way of they're precious profits — that is, unless they literally own the town . If it's a horror story , they may be keeping a Madwoman in the Attic , as well as hiding much worse things from the public eye. Their underlings will be either corrupt themselves or clueless , and potentially made up of the tyrants relatives — areas that tolerate these folks generally don't get reformed solely from the inside, with the newcomer in the town being the one who usually lights the sparks in the first place. Hitting them in the pocketbook is an assured way to rile their goat, but in most stories (especially those that Homage classic shows) this will just lead to a brutal escalation culminating in a huge Final Battle that will put the tyrant in jail or the morgue. Walking the Earth shows, particularly those set in the modern day, run into a lot of these. And virtually every adventure series or cop show made in the 1970s, whether it be Cannon or The Six Million Dollar Man or Wonder Woman had at least one episode featuring this sort of character. noreallife
